4: wayyēlek šimšôn wayyilkoḏ šəlōš-mêʾôt šûʿālîm wayyiqqaḥ lappîḏîm wayyipeŋ zānāḇ ʾel-zānāḇ — 'Samson went and caught three hundred foxes and took torches; he turned tail to tail and put one torch between each pair of tails.' šûʿāl (H7776, fox/jackal — both animals are intended by the word) — the catching of 300 animals is itself a Samson feat. lappîḏ (H3940, torch/firebrand — the same word as in Judges 7:16 where Gideon's men carry torches) — firebrands between paired fox tails would have created uncontrollable running fire through standing grain. The agricultural sabotage destroys the Philistine harvest at precisely the wrong time — vindictive, effective, and proportionally catastrophic.
11: wayyōʾmərû lô maddûaʿ ʿālîtā ʿālênû wayyōʾmer lāhem kaʾăšer ʿāśû lî kēn ʿāśîtî lāhem — 'They said to him: Do you not know that the Philistines rule over us? What is this that you have done to us? He said: As they did to me, so I have done to them.' māšal (H4910, to rule/have dominion) — the Philistines 'rule over' Israel; Israel has internalized the oppressor's authority as normal and resents any disruption to it. Samson's 'measure for measure' (kaʾăšer... kēn) ethic is lex talionis (Leviticus 24:19-20) — exact reciprocity. But the real irony is that Israel ties up their own deliverer to hand him to the Philistines (vv12-13).
14: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wattihyênāh hāʾăḇôtîm ʾăšer ʿal-zərōʿāyw kappîštîm ʾăšer bāʿărû ḇāʾēš — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and the ropes on his arms became like flax burned with fire.' For the third time in Samson's story ṣālaḥ rûaḥ YHWH — the Spirit rushes upon him. pišṭāh (H6593, flax/linen thread) + bāʿar ḇāʾēš (burning with fire) — the ropes that would bind him dissolve like burning flax. The metaphor captures instantaneous dissolution: fire makes flax linen structurally worthless immediately. This third Spirit-empowerment in Judges 14-15 establishes the pattern that will be broken in Judges 16.
15: wayyimṣāʾ lḥî-ḥāmôr ṭəriyyāh wayyišlaḥ yāḏô wayyiqqāḥēhû wayyak-bô ʾelep ʾîš — 'He found a fresh jawbone of a donkey, reached out and took it, and struck down a thousand men with it.' ləḥî-ḥāmôr (H3895 H2543, jaw/cheek of a donkey) — the jawbone weapon is deliberately humble: not a sword, spear, or club but an agricultural animal's discarded bone. ṭəriyyāh (H2961, fresh/new — not yet dried and brittle) — the moisture in a fresh jawbone gives it structural integrity as a weapon. The contrast with conventional weapons echoes the theological principle of Judges: YHWH delivers through inadequate means (tent peg, jawbone) to make his exclusive agency clear.
18: wayyiṣmāʾ məʾōḏ wayyiqrāʾ ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾattāh nātattā bəyaḏ-ʿaḇdəkā ʾet-hayyəšûʿāh hagdôlāh hazzōʾt — 'He was very thirsty, and he called to YHWH and said: You have given this great salvation by the hand of your servant.' Samson prays — one of the rare moments of direct divine address in his story. yəšûʿāh gəḏôlāh (H3444 H1419, great deliverance/salvation) — the same vocabulary as YHWH's covenant saving acts throughout the OT. Even Samson, who often acts independently, must acknowledge that the victory was YHWH's gift. waʾattāh ʾāmût baṣṣāmāʾ — 'and now shall I die of thirst?' — after the great victory, ordinary thirst threatens the life YHWH just used for deliverance. The prayer is answered immediately (v19).
19: wayyibqaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hammakteš ʾăšer ballĕḥî wayyēṣəʾû mimmennû mayim wayyišt wayyāšob rûḥô wayyiḥî — 'God split open the hollow place that is in Lehi, and water came out from it, and he drank, and his spirit returned and he revived.' bāqaʿ (H1234, to split/break open — used of the Exodus sea, Psalms 78:13; of the rock in the wilderness, Psalms 78:15-16) — God provides water in the wilderness pattern; the jawbone victory is followed by a water-from-rock moment. makteš (H4388, hollow/mortar — the place of grinding, also a bowl-shaped depression in rock) becomes the spring. The place is named ʿên haqqôrēʾ (H5879, 'Spring of the One Who Calls/Called') — a place-name encoding Samson's prayer, preserved in geography as testimony to answered prayer.
1: wayhî miyāmîm biqṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm wayyiqqōḏ šimšôn ʾet-ʾištô bigəḏî-ʿizzîm wayyōʾmer ʾābōʾāh ʾel-ʾištî haḥāḏərāh — 'After some time, at the time of wheat harvest, Samson visited his wife with a young goat, and he said: I will go into my wife in the chamber.' The wheat harvest timing (qəṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm) places this return in late spring. gəḏî-ʿizzîm (H1423 H5795, young goat) — a young goat was a standard reconciliation/visit gift in ancient Near Eastern custom (Genesis 38:17 Judah to Tamar; Genesis 38:20). Samson returns expecting to resume the marriage, apparently unaware that his wife has been given away. The goat gift implies he is making a goodwill approach, not coming in anger — which makes the father's disclosure in v2 even more devastating.
2: wayyōʾmer ʾāḇîhā ʾāmōr ʾāmartî kî-śānōʾ śənēʾtāh — 'But her father said: I really thought that you utterly hated her.' śānōʾ śənēʾtāh (infinitive absolute + finite verb — the intensified form: 'utterly hated') — the father explains his decision: Samson's angry departure after the wedding feast (Judges 14:19) was read as marital abandonment, and under ancient Near Eastern custom an abandoned wife could be given to another. wāʾettənāh ləmērēʿăkā hălōʾ ʾăḥōtāh haqqəṭannāh ṭôḇāh mimmenāh — 'so I gave her to your companion. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead.' The offer of the younger sister is a face-saving consolation prize; Samson's response rejects it and frames his response as righteous retaliation.
3: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn niqqêtî happaʿam mippəlištîm kî-ʾōśeh ʾănî ʿimmāhem rāʿāh — 'Samson said to them: This time I shall be blameless with regard to the Philistines, when I do them harm.' niqqêtî (H5352 Niphal, to be blameless/innocent/acquitted — from nāqāh H5352) — Samson declares his retaliatory action to be morally justified: he is making himself 'clean' or 'acquitted' by responding in kind. The logic is lex talionis (they wronged him; he is entitled to respond). Whether the declaration is theologically legitimate is ambiguous — YHWH does not comment. The narrative uses Samson's personal grievances as the vehicle for national-scale Philistine punishment, but Samson's framing is entirely self-referential.
5: wayyabbeʿ-ʾēš balappîḏîm wayšallaḥ bəqāmôt pəlištîm wayyabʿar miggādîš wəʿaḏ-qāmāh wəʿaḏ-kerem zayit — 'He lit the torches and set the foxes free into the standing grain of the Philistines, and burned up the shocks and the standing grain and also the olive orchards.' gādîš (H1430, shock of grain — grain already cut and stacked for drying) and qāmāh (H7054, standing grain — still unharvested) and kerem zayit (H3754 H2132, olive orchard) — three stages of agricultural production are destroyed: stored grain, unharvested grain, and the perennial orchards that take decades to mature. The olive trees are the most severe loss: destroying an olive grove sets back a community's food supply for a generation. This is total agricultural warfare.
6: wayyōʾmərû pəlištîm mî ʿāśāh zōʾt wayyōʾmərû šimšôn ḥătan hattimānî kî lāqaḥ ʾet-ʾištô wayyittnāh ləmērēʿēhû wayyaʿălû pəlištîm wayyiśrəpû ʾôtāh wəʾet-ʾāḇîhā bāʾēš — 'The Philistines said: Who has done this? They said: Samson, the son-in-law of the Timnite, because he took his wife and gave her to his companion. And the Philistines came up and burned her and her father with fire.' The Philistines retaliate against the woman and her father for Samson's action — punishing the innocent parties rather than Samson himself. The burning of the woman (the very threat made in 14:15 to extract the riddle) comes full circle: the threat used to coerce her is now executed against her. The cycle of vengeance expands: Philistines burned Samson → Samson burned their fields → Philistines burned his wife → Samson strikes them again (v7-8).
7: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn ʾim-tāʿăśûn kāzōʾt kî ʾim-niqqamtî bākem wəʾaḥar ʾeḥdāl — 'Samson said to them: If you do this, I will surely take revenge on you, and after that I will stop.' niqqam (H5358, to avenge/take revenge — the same root as Samson's final prayer in Judges 16:28) — Samson's life is structured as a cycle of revenge declarations followed by devastating acts of force. Each declaration escalates the conflict. The phrase wəʾaḥar ʾeḥdāl — 'and after that I will stop' — is Samson's pattern of self-imposed limits that he never actually keeps; the conflict always continues because his actions generate new retaliation.
8: wayyak ʾôtām šôq ʿal-yārēk makkāh gəḏôlāh wayyēreḏ wayyēšeḇ bəsəʿîp-sela haʿêṭām — 'He struck them hip and thigh with a great blow, and he went down and stayed in the cleft of the rock of Etam.' šôq ʿal-yārēk (H7785 H3409, shin/thigh upon thigh — an idiom of uncertain origin, possibly from wrestling or battle, describing a thorough, total defeat: 'piling blow on blow') — 'hip and thigh' is a unique idiomatic expression meaning a comprehensive, unreserved slaughter. Samson then withdraws to the rock of Etam (sela haʿêṭām), a natural fortress in Judah's territory — the pattern of retreat after a major act of force that leaves the political problem unresolved.
9: wayyaʿălû pəlištîm wayyaḥănû bîhûḏāh wayyinnāṭəšû bəlaḥî — 'Then the Philistines came up and encamped in Judah and made a raid on Lehi.' nāṭaš (H5203, to spread out/deploy/raid — used of armies spreading across terrain) — the Philistine incursion into Judah proper over a single man demonstrates both Samson's military significance to them and Judah's political vulnerability. laḥî (H3896, Lehi — meaning 'jawbone') is the place name that will receive its etiology from the upcoming battle (v9-17). The geographic specificity grounds the narrative: this is territory history, not legend.
10: wayyōʾmərû ʾanšê yəhûḏāh lāmāh ʿălîtem ʿālênû wayyōʾmərû laʾăsōr ʾet-šimšôn ʿālînû — 'The men of Judah said: Why have you come up against us? They said: We have come up to bind Samson, to do to him as he did to us.' The Philistines' answer is specific: they want Samson, not territory. The men of Judah's compliance in v12-13 — binding their own deliverer to hand him to the enemy — is one of Judges' most disturbing moments: Israel has internalized subjugation so thoroughly that they participate in suppressing their own liberation. The oppressor controls them not just by force but by persuading them to police themselves.
12: wayyōʾmərû lô laʾăsōr-kā yāraḏnû lōsōr-kā ûlitttənkā bəyaḏ-pəlištîm wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn hišāḇəʿû lî pen-tipgəʿûn-bî ʾattem — 'They said to him: We have come down to bind you, that we may give you into the hands of the Philistines. And Samson said to them: Swear to me that you will not attack me yourselves.' Samson's one condition — that his own people not strike him — reveals his trust calculus: he trusts Judah's oath more than the Philistines' restraint. hišāḇəʿû (H7650 Hiphil, to cause to swear) — Samson administers an oath to his own countrymen before surrendering to them. The scene is a grotesque inversion of military protocol: a deliverer being bound and surrendered by the people he came to deliver.
13: wayyōʾmərû lô lēʾmōr lōʾ ʾim-ʾāsōr neʾesārkā ûnətatnākā bəyāḏām wəhārōg lōʾ nahargekkā wayyaʾasrūhû bišənê ʿăbōtîm ḥăḏāšîm wayyaʿăluhû min-hassālaʿ — 'They said to him: No; we will only bind you and give you into their hand. We will not kill you. So they bound him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.' ʿăbōt ḥăḏāšîm (H5688 H2319, new ropes) — the 'new ropes' detail anticipates Judges 16:11 where the same phrase appears in Delilah's second trap. The two new ropes that bind Samson here will dissolve like flax (v14) — the same pattern established in Judges 16 in reverse. Judah binds their deliverer with new ropes; the Spirit dissolves them. YHWH overrules Israel's complicity.
16: wayyōʾmer šimšôn bilḥî haḥāmôr ḥămôr ḥămōrātayim bilḥî haḥāmôr hikkêtî ʾelep ʾîš — 'Samson said: With the jawbone of a donkey, heaps upon heaps, with the jawbone of a donkey I have struck down a thousand men.' bilḥî haḥāmôr (with the jawbone of the donkey) — the victory song is a wordplay: ḥāmôr (H2543, donkey) sounds like ḥōmer (H2563, heap/pile of grain). ḥămôr ḥămōrātayim may be read as 'heaps, two heaps' — a doubling that intensifies the count. The boast song is Samson's only recorded speech-act of praise, and its grammar is military: first-person victorious declaration. Compare Deborah's song (Judges 5): Samson's victory song is self-referential where Deborah's is YHWH-referential.
17: wayhî kəkallōtô lədabbēr wayyašleḵ haləḥî miyyāḏô wayyiqrāʾ lamāqôm hahûʾ rāmat-laḥî — 'When he had finished speaking, he threw away the jawbone, and that place was called Ramath-lehi.' rāmat-laḥî (H7437, 'height of the jawbone' or 'throwing of the jawbone') — the place name encodes the weapon's use. In Judges, place-naming is a consistent narrative practice marking sites of divine intervention: Beer-lahai-roi (Genesis 16:14), Penuel (Genesis 32:31), Bochim (Judges 2:5), and here. The physical jawbone becomes a geographic marker of YHWH's deliverance, preserved in the landscape as testimony.
20: wayyišpōṭ ʾet-yiśrāʾēl bîmê pəlištîm ʿeśrîm šānāh — 'And he judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years.' The twenty-year judgeship formula (ʿeśrîm šānāh) closes the Samson cycle's second act (cf. 16:31 where the same formula closes his career). The formula is deliberately minimal compared to other judges: no 'the land had rest for X years,' no summary of peace. The phrasing 'in the days of the Philistines' acknowledges that Samson's judgeship did not end Philistine domination — unlike Othniel, Deborah, or Gideon, Samson did not liberate the land. He harassed the Philistines without resolving the crisis, leaving the work for Samuel and David.
